title: Pricing Strategy Workshop
summary: A step-by-step process for setting, validating, and adjusting your pricing. Most founders underprice — this helps you find the right number.
whenToUseIt: When launching a new product, entering a new market, or when you suspect your pricing is wrong.

steps.title: Understand your cost floor
steps.description: Calculate the minimum you must charge to break even on each unit/customer. Include: direct costs, indirect costs, and desired margin. This is your floor — never price below this.

steps.title: Research competitor pricing
steps.description: Map pricing for 5 competitors. Note: pricing model, price points, what is included at each tier, and whether they publish pricing.

steps.title: Quantify the value you deliver
steps.description: What is the measurable outcome your product creates? Time saved, revenue generated, cost reduced, risk avoided. Your price should be 10-20% of the value you create.
steps.tips: If your product saves a customer $100K/year, charging $10K/year is a 10x ROI for them.
steps.aiPrompt: My product [description] helps [customer type] achieve [outcome]. Help me quantify the dollar value of this outcome. Then suggest a pricing range based on delivering 5-10x ROI.

steps.title: Design your pricing tiers
steps.description: Create 2-3 tiers. Each tier should serve a distinct customer segment. The middle tier should be where most customers land.

steps.title: Test with 5 customers or prospects
steps.description: Show your pricing to 5 real people. Ask: At this price, would you buy? What would make it a no-brainer? If everyone says yes immediately, you are probably too cheap.

steps.title: Set your price and review date
steps.description: Set your price. Commit to it for 90 days. Set a calendar reminder to review.
steps.tips: It is almost always better to start higher and discount down than to start low and try to raise.

expectedOutcome: A defensible pricing strategy with defined tiers, tested with real customers, grounded in value delivered.
commonMistakes: Pricing based only on cost — cost-plus pricing ignores value
Copying competitor pricing without understanding your differentiation
Having one price for everyone — different segments have different willingness to pay
Never raising prices — if you have not raised prices in 12 months, you are probably underpricing
